ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken class; no specific fried chicken wing subclass exists. FOODON_03302051 used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked fried chicken meat broadly; no wing-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Used as closest defensible match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for fried chicken wing identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 1 piece (80 g), providing around 232 kcal, 14.4 g protein, 14.9 g fat, and 10.0 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Chicken Wing — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken wing, fried, coated (NDB No. 05298) to supplement HPB data where HPB did not report these fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried chicken (GI ~65)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for fried chicken wing with batter exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: battered/breaded fried chicken, which appears in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables with a GI of approximately 65 (medium). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (65 × 12.48 g carbohydrate per 100g × 80g serving) / 100 = 8.1 g GL per 80g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 616 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-sodium threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit fried chicken wing due to its sodium content of 616 mg/100 g, which exceeds the recommended upper limit; if consumed, restrict to a single small piece (80 g) and avoid additional high-sodium condiments or dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined batter coating contributes moderate carbohydrate load (12.5 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.43 g/100 g), and high total fat (18.6 g/100 g) with saturated fat (5 g/100 g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit fried chicken wing to occasional small portions (1 piece, 80 g), remove the batter coating where possible to reduce refined carbohydrate and fat intake, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.6 g/100 g), saturated fat (5 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), cholesterol (90 mg/100 g), and sodium (616 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of fried chicken wing due to its combination of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and high sodium, all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated blood pressure; if consumed, limit to one small piece (80 g) on rare occasions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 616 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold for tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried chicken wing as deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, and the high sodium content (616 mg/100 g) may worsen RA-related tissue swelling and joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried chicken wing is a popular deep-fried dish consisting of a whole chicken wing coated in a seasoned wheat flour or cornstarch batter and submerged in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy while the interior remains juicy and tender. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, the marinade typically includes soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, ginger, and sometimes fish sauce, imparting a savoury umami flavour with a lightly spiced aroma. The batter creates a crunchy shell that contrasts with the moist, flavourful chicken meat underneath. Per 100 g, fried chicken wing provides 290 kcal, 18 g protein, 18.6 g total fat (including 5 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat), 12.5 g carbohydrate, and 616 mg sodium. The high sodium content (616 mg/100 g) makes this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or rheumatoid arthritis. The combination of saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol (90 mg/100 g) raises cardiovascular concern. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the refined batter coating contributes a moderate glycaemic load and the high fat content may worsen insulin resistance. Fried chicken wing is widely consumed as a snack, side dish, or main course across hawker centres, fast-food outlets, and home kitchens in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Chinese-influenced culinary diaspora.

Can I eat fried chicken wing if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for fried coated chicken. GI estimated from the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (battered/breaded fried chicken, GI ~65). All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific fried chicken wing preparation is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for battered fried chicken wing as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this food item and could not be reliably confirmed from a single approved secondary source without risk of misattribution. 3. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo); this is plausible given the savoury marinade profile with no added sugar declared. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo); this is expected for a deep-fried meat product. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried chicken from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried chicken wing with batter exists in the database as of 2026. 5. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' present in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as it is not applicable to a battered deep-fried chicken wing product. 6. Only one portion size is flagged as default (1 piece, 80 g); the input contained multiple portions all marked is_default=true, which is inconsistent — only the most representative single-serve portion is retained as default. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.25 g/100 g is retained from the input (sourced from grabfood in the original meta); this value has been cross-checked against USDA data for fried chicken which reports small but non-zero trans fat from frying oils, and is considered plausible.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input. Identified issues: (a) input GI value was 0 with no approved source — replaced with analogous estimate of 65 from University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken; (b) input health_context used 'diabetes' key instead of required 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' — corrected; (c) cardiovascular_disease condition was missing from input — added; (d) multilingual names were absent — added Chinese (simplified/traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi per G6 Singaporean dish rule; (e) aliases_by_language was absent — built from scratch; (f) ontology was null — added defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes with gap notes; (g) 'wholegrain' dietary tag removed as inapplicable; (h) -1 sentinel values documented in notes per G3 rule; (i) sugar_g=0.0 and vitamin_c_mg=0.0 justified as confirmed zero per HPB Foodo in notes.

Iteration 2: Self-scored draft. Validator feedback indicated g5_ontology_honesty failed because foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type were 'approximate' instead of 'closest_parent', 'exact', or 'none'. Corrected these values to 'closest_parent' as they represent the closest defensible parent classes. All 8 Q criteria pass. All 6 hard gates pass. Confirmed aliases consistency: all aliases_by_language string values present verbatim in flat aliases array. Score 8/8 — PASS confirmed.
